title: GPU VRAM Guide
summary: Understand GPU memory demand, model size, batch size, resolution, analytics load, and safe margin.

sections.title: What this tool does
body: This tool estimates whether a GPU has enough VRAM for the intended workload.
bullets: Use it as a planning and sanity-check aid.
Use it to document assumptions before implementation.
Use it to catch design pressure before it becomes field trouble.

sections.title: When to use it
body: Use it for AI inference, video analytics, machine learning experiments, rendering workloads, or GPU-accelerated compute tasks.
bullets: Early design planning.
Expansion review.
Existing-system cleanup or validation.
Explaining tradeoffs to a client, stakeholder, or internal team.

sections.title: Input descriptions
body: These descriptions explain what each input represents in practical compute and infrastructure planning terms.

items.label: Model / Workload Size
items.description: The memory footprint of the GPU workload.
items.whyItMatters: Larger models consume more VRAM before live data is processed.
items.commonMistake: Only checking GPU compute power and ignoring memory.

items.label: Batch Size
items.description: How many items are processed at once.
items.whyItMatters: Larger batches usually increase VRAM demand.
items.commonMistake: Increasing batch size without checking memory headroom.

items.label: Resolution
items.description: The input size or visual resolution being processed.
items.commonMistake: Testing at low resolution and deploying at high resolution.

items.label: Stream Count
items.description: How many simultaneous video or data streams use the GPU.
items.whyItMatters: More streams can multiply VRAM requirements.
items.commonMistake: Sizing for one stream and scaling without recalculating.

items.label: VRAM Capacity
items.whyItMatters: VRAM capacity determines whether the workload can fit.
items.commonMistake: Assuming faster GPU means enough memory.

items.label: Headroom
items.description: VRAM reserved for overhead, drivers, buffers, and growth.
items.whyItMatters: Headroom reduces failure risk during bursts or model changes.
items.commonMistake: Running VRAM at the edge of capacity.

sections.title: How to read the results
body: Healthy means VRAM appears adequate. Watch means workload settings or growth should be reviewed. Risk means the job may fail, spill, or require a smaller model/batch/resolution.
bullets: Healthy: the design appears manageable based on the entered assumptions.
Watch: the design may still work, but the assumptions deserve review.
Risk: add capacity, simplify the design, or validate the design more carefully before implementation.

sections.title: Common mistakes
body: Most compute planning problems come from sizing one resource in isolation while ignoring the way CPU, memory, storage, network, power, and growth interact.
bullets: Sizing only for average load instead of peak behavior.
Ignoring headroom, background tasks, maintenance, and growth.
Trusting theoretical maximums as if they are sustained real-world capacity.
Failing to document assumptions before the system is built.

sections.title: Planning assumptions
body: This guide explains the planning logic behind the tool. It does not replace manufacturer documentation, platform-specific limits, application requirements, or project-specific engineering judgment.
bullets: Vendor specifications still matter.
Application behavior may change the final requirement.
Site conditions and operating procedures may affect the design.
A design with headroom is usually easier to support and grow.

sections.title: Professional responsibility
body: ScopedLabs tools are planning aids. They help surface assumptions and risk, but they do not replace formal engineering review, manufacturer validation, application testing, or project-specific design responsibility.
bullets: Validate against the actual hardware, platform, and software being deployed.
Confirm stakeholder expectations and operating requirements.
Document assumptions before implementation.
